meta.title: F90 M5 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every BMW M5 F90 engine — S63 4.4-litre twin-turbo V8, standard, Competition, CS. Real UK costs and honest reliability data. Get free quotes from 20+ vetted BMW specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW M5 F90 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned S63 engine typically costs £6,000–£15,000 supply only [BMW-QUOTE], depending on the variant — the standard M5 sits at £6,000–£12,000, the Competition at £6,500–£13,000, and the CS at £8,000–£15,000. Fitting adds £910–£1,170. A full engine rebuild can exceed £20,000. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which F90 M5 powertrain is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: All F90 M5s use the same S63 4.4-litre twin-turbo V8 — just in different states of tune. The engine is fundamentally robust, but the plastic coolant expansion tank is a known weakness that can fail and cause coolant leaks. A billet aluminium replacement is strongly recommended for any F90 M5.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW M5 F90?

acceptedAnswer.text: The plastic coolant expansion tank is the single biggest weak point — when it fails, coolant leaks onto the rear ignition coils and spark plugs, causing misfires. Oil leaks from the valve cover and oil filter housing are common. Fuel pump issues have been reported in some cars, particularly after long high-speed driving.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW M5 F90 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — the S63 V8 is a proven, robust engine. However, it's a high-performance engine that requires diligent maintenance. The coolant expansion tank should be replaced with a billet aluminium unit as preventative maintenance. Oil changes should be frequent, and the cooling system should be inspected regularly.

mainEntity.name: Which F90 M5 powertrain should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No powertrain outright — the CS is the most expensive and rare, but all share the same fundamental engine. A car without documented coolant expansion tank replacement is a risk. The Competition is the sweet spot in the range. Avoid any car with poor service history or obvious coolant leaks.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my F90 M5 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Coolant loss or misfires (coolant expansion tank failure), oil leaks (valve cover/oil filter housing), stalling or hard starting (fuel pump issues), or persistent warning lights are the key warning signs. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing an F90 M5 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a £35,000 M5, a £6,000–£12,000 S63 recon is 17–34% — well within our 70% BMW threshold. For a Competition at £35,000+, a £6,500–£13,000 recon is 19–37% — a repair decision, not a scrap question. For a CS at £60,000+, a £8,000–£15,000 recon is 13–25% — a manageable cost for a collector's car. Value the car first; budget for the coolant tank immediately.

mainEntity.name: How long do S63 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: With proper preventative maintenance, the S63 can exceed 100,000 miles. The coolant expansion tank should be replaced as soon as possible. Oil changes should be frequent (every 5,000–8,000 miles). Many well-maintained F90 M5s are now well over 50,000 miles and still going strong.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the F90, F10, and G90 M5?

acceptedAnswer.text: The F90 (2017–2023) is the sixth-generation M5, succeeding the F10 (2011–2017). The F90 was the first M5 with xDrive all-wheel drive and the last purely petrol-powered M5. The G90 (2024–present) is the seventh-generation M5 — it uses a hybrid V8 powertrain with 727 bhp. The F10 was rear-wheel drive only with a similar S63 V8. The F90 offers the best balance of performance, usability, and character.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my F90 M5 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; the engine code is also stamped on the cylinder block. For petrol: S63B44T4 (standard M5), S63B44B (M5 Competition), S63B44B (M5 CS). Tell us your variant and registration year and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted BMW specialists will confirm it for free.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: All ratings are provisional — no dedicated Engine Page exists for the S63 engine family. The S63 is a proven powerhouse — the F90's engine is an evolution of the S63 found in the F10 M5, with upgraded turbos and revised cooling. It won multiple awards for its performance. However, it has known reliability concerns: coolant expansion tank failures, oil leaks, and fuel pump issues. The S63 is fundamentally robust but requires diligent maintenance — these are high-performance, high-stress engines.

overview.intro: The F90 is the sixth generation of the BMW M5, an executive super-saloon produced from 2017 to 2023. It was the first M5 to feature all-wheel drive (xDrive) and the last purely petrol-powered M5 before the G90 hybrid. The F90 is built on the BMW CLAR platform and shares its underpinnings with the G30 5 Series. The engine is the S63 4.4-litre twin-turbo V8 — a development of the F10 M5's engine with upgraded turbos and revised cooling. The line‑up includes the standard M5 (600 PS), the M5 Competition (625 PS), and the limited-run M5 CS (635 PS). A 2020 Life Cycle Impulse brought revised styling, iDrive 7, and subtle chassis updates. The F90 was discontinued in 2023 and succeeded by the G90 hybrid M5.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: BMW CLAR • Predecessor: BMW M5 (F10) • Successor: BMW M5 (G90) • Sibling body styles in this generation: G30 5 Series, G31 5 Series Touring
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The 5 Series nameplate generated significant UK engine enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED], with demand for the F90 M5 concentrated on the Competition model based on market patterns.

rows.ownershipVerdict: High Performance, High Cost — budget for preventative maintenance
Sweet Spot — more power, similar running costs

economicsRule.title: The F90 M5 rule
economicsRule.text: A £6,000–£13,000 S63 recon against a £30,000–£60,000 M5 sits at 10–43% of the car's value — well within our 70% BMW scrap threshold. For a Competition at £35,000+, a £6,500–£13,000 recon is 19–37% — a repair decision, not a scrap question. For a CS at £60,000+, a £8,000–£15,000 recon is 13–25% — a manageable cost for a collector's car. These are high-value performance cars; the replacement cost is a manageable proportion of the vehicle's worth. But beware of the coolant expansion tank — budget for a billet aluminium replacement if buying used.

keyTakeaways.question: Which F90 M5 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: Standard M5 — lowest power, slightly cheaper parts, but still expensive.

keyTakeaways.question: Which F90 M5 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: M5 CS — rare parts, specialist labour, eye-watering costs.

keyTakeaways.question: Which F90 M5 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: M5 Competition — the sweet spot in the range, more power than standard, similar running costs.

problems.title: Coolant Expansion Tank Failure (All F90 M5s)
problems.recommendation: If you're buying any F90 M5, budget for a billet aluminium expansion tank replacement immediately — £200–£500 is dramatically cheaper than the £4,000+ repair that follows a failure. This is the single most important preventative measure for any F90 M5 owner.

problems.title: Oil Leaks (All F90 M5s)
problems.recommendation: Have the valve cover and oil filter housing inspected at every service — a £500–£1,500 gasket replacement is cheaper than the £5,000+ replacement that follows a catastrophic oil loss.

problems.title: Fuel Pump Issues (All F90 M5s)
problems.recommendation: If your M5 shows signs of stalling or hard starting, have the fuel system inspected immediately — a £400–£1,000 fuel pump replacement is cheaper than the £5,000+ replacement that follows a catastrophic failure.

replacementCosts.h2: F90 M5 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are F90 only. The M5 Competition was introduced in 2018, and the M5 CS in 2021. The LCI facelift (2020) brought revised styling and iDrive 7 but no major mechanical changes to the engine. The F90 was the last purely petrol-powered M5 — the G90 successor uses a hybrid V8.
